title: Cedarwood and lime soy jar candle
subtitle: A 200 ml container candle with a warm woody base and a bright citrus top note
excerpt: Cedarwood essential oil forms the base note of this soy jar candle, with lime essential oil cutting through as a bright citrus top. The two work together in the wax without the lime overpowering the cedarwood on the cold throw. A standard 200 ml glass jar with a CD-12 wick gives a 25 to 30-hour burn.
sourceNotes: Soy wax container candle technique: candle wick sizing guide (Candlescience, 2023) for CD-12 in a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar. Fragrance load at 8% by wax weight: standard maximum for GW 464 soy wax; higher rates risk sweating or poor flame performance. Cedarwood EO (Cedrus atlantica or Juniperus virginiana) and lime EO (Citrus aurantifolia) are both toxic to cats; note in the safety section.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 200 ml jar candle (130 g soy wax fill)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale by multiplying all weights by the number of jars. Keep one pre-tabbed wick per jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 3 to 5 days before burning for maximum fragrance throw.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Effectively indefinite for the wax. Fragrance fades after 2 years. Keep the lid on between burns to slow the scent loss. Discard if the wax surface becomes discoloured or the wick base shows corrosion.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first burn of a candle sets the wax memory: the diameter of the melt pool on the first burn becomes the maximum diameter it will reach on all subsequent burns. Burning until the pool reaches the jar edge prevents tunnelling.
The temperature at which liquid wax is poured into the container. Soy container wax is poured at 55 to 65°C; too hot and the wax pulls away from the sides; too cool and it sets unevenly.

content.text: Cedarwood and lime is a pairing that works because the two are on opposite ends of the fragrance spectrum: cedarwood is warm, slow-releasing, and earthy; lime is sharp, citrus, and fast-evaporating. In a soy container candle, cedarwood holds well in the wax through the burn; lime carries on the cold throw (the scent from an unlit candle) more than through the hot throw (when burning). Together they give a clean, nature-adjacent scent profile. This candle uses essential oils rather than fragrance oil; the cold throw is softer than a synthetic blend, but the essential oils are the real thing. Cedarwood and lime essential oils are toxic to cats; keep the candle out of rooms where cats spend time.

items.prepNote: Container-blend soy wax; GW 464 or equivalent
3% of wax weight; cold-pressed lime peel oil, phototoxic on skin but safe in a room candle
CD-12 for a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar

content.text: Never melt wax over a direct flame; always use a double boiler or the candle pouring pitcher in a water bath. Place the soy wax in the pouring pitcher and set in a pan of barely simmering water. Heat, stirring occasionally, until the wax is fully melted and reaches 80°C. Remove from the heat and let it cool.

While the wax cools, stick the metal sustainer tab of the pre-tabbed wick to the centre of the jar base. Use a small amount of melted wax or a wick sticker. Thread the wick through the centring bar and lay the bar across the rim of the jar so the wick hangs plumb in the centre.
When the wax has cooled to 60°C, add the cedarwood and lime essential oils and stir for 2 minutes so the oils are fully incorporated. Pour at
: between 55 and 65°C. Pour in a slow, steady stream down the side of the jar. Leave a 5 to 10 mm gap at the top. Leave undisturbed until fully set; do not move the candle while the wax is liquid.
Leave the candle to cure for at least 1 day before burning; 3 to 5 days gives the best fragrance throw. Trim the wick to 5 mm before each burn.

. Burn the candle until the melt pool reaches the full diameter of the jar, which takes 3 to 4 hours for a 200 ml jar. Extinguish before the last 10 mm of wax. Do not burn in a draught; an uneven flame tunnels the wick down the centre and leaves wax on the sides.

Keep the candle with its lid on between burns to preserve the fragrance. In a cool, dark place, the wax is stable for 5 years or more; the essential oil fragrance fades noticeably after around 2 years. Toxic to cats; keep out of rooms where cats are present.
